---
title: "Joint Distributions, Covariance, and Correlation"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"     # data points
LINE   = "#c1121f"     # fitted line / key curve
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / means
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PURPLE = "#6a4c93"
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the seventh stop in **Probability
> Foundations**, the root track of the series. It assumes you have read
> [PROB-05 *Expectation: The Long-Run Average*] — where $E[X]$, the **law of the
> unconscious statistician** $E[g(X)]$, and the all-important **linearity of
> expectation** were built — and [PROB-06 *Variance, Standard Deviation, and
> Variance as a Sum of Squares*], where $\operatorname{Var}(X)=E[(X-\mu)^2]$ and
> the computational identity $\operatorname{Var}(X)=E[X^2]-(E[X])^2$ were derived.
> Until now every random variable has lived alone. Here we put **two** of them in
> the same room and ask how they move together. It sets up [PROB-08 *Independence
> and the i.i.d. Sample*] (where zero covariance and independence finally part
> ways for good), [PROB-17 *Random Vectors, Mean Vectors, and Covariance
> Matrices*] (the covariance of this article becomes a *matrix*), and
> [PROB-18 *Linear Transforms of Random Vectors*]. Downstream it is consumed by
> Regression (R2/R6), the Geometry of Data track (covariance as a Gram matrix),
> and Unsupervised Learning (PCA).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A single random variable is described by one distribution; *two* of them require a
**joint distribution** — one object over the plane that says how likely each pair of
values is. From it we recover each variable alone (the **marginals**) by summing or
integrating the other away, and we measure their co-movement with the **covariance**
$\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=E[(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)]$. This article derives the covariance, its
shortcut $\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=E[XY]-E[X]E[Y]$, its **bilinearity**, and the identity
that the rest of statistics runs on,
$$\operatorname{Var}(X+Y)=\operatorname{Var}(X)+\operatorname{Var}(Y)+2\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y).$$
Because covariance carries the units of $X$ times the units of $Y$, it cannot be compared
across problems, so we standardize it into the **correlation** $\rho$, prove it lives in
$[-1,1]$ using nothing heavier than "a variance is never negative," and show $\rho=\pm 1$
means an exact line. The single claim the article defends is that **covariance measures
*linear* co-movement and nothing else** — which is why its most famous trap,
*zero covariance does not imply independence*, is not a paradox but a definition working
exactly as designed.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
To describe two random variables together you need their **joint distribution**; from it,
each one alone is a **marginal**. Their co-movement is the **covariance**
$\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=E[(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)]$ — the average product of their
simultaneous deviations from their own means. Strip its units away by dividing by the two
standard deviations and you get the **correlation** $\rho\in[-1,1]$, a pure number that is
$\pm 1$ exactly when the points fall on a perfect line. But covariance sees **only the
linear part** of a relationship: two variables can be tightly, deterministically linked
and still have covariance zero.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The covariance will at first look like an arbitrary
"average product of deviations," but by the end you should feel why it is the only natural
choice: it is the cross-term that pops out when you square a sum, the off-diagonal entry of
a variance, and the exact generalization of @eq-var-sum-of-squares from one variable to
two. Hold onto the last sentence of the Core — *covariance sees only the linear part* —
because it is simultaneously the reason covariance is so useful and the reason it is so
dangerous (@sec-zero-cov).

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced a joint object {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **how do we describe two uncertain
quantities *together*, and how do we put a single number on whether they move with each
other?** Every definition below answers some piece of that.

## One distribution is not enough

Up to now a random variable came with one distribution and we were done. But the questions
that *matter* are almost never about one quantity in isolation. A pension fund does not
care about one stock's return; it cares whether its stocks crash *together*. An
epidemiologist does not care about height or weight alone; the interesting science is in
their relationship. A regression — the workhorse of the entire applied series — is
literally a statement about how an outcome $Y$ moves with a predictor $X$.

The moment you have two quantities $X$ and $Y$, knowing each one's distribution separately
is **not enough to answer questions about the pair**. Suppose $X$ is "did it rain today"
and $Y$ is "did I bring an umbrella." You can know that it rains 30% of days and that I
carry an umbrella 35% of days, and *still* have no idea whether I carry the umbrella
*when* it rains. The two separate distributions are silent about the link. We need a single
object that records the probability of each *combination* of values at once. That object is
the **joint distribution**, and this article is the story of how to build it, how to pull
the individual variables back out of it, and how to compress "do they move together" into
one interpretable number.

## What came before, and why it was inadequate

The thread that produced covariance is the same astronomy-and-data thread that produced
least squares. Francis Galton, studying heredity in the 1880s, plotted children's heights
against their parents' and saw an unmistakable *tilt* in the cloud: tall parents tended to
have tall children. He needed to **quantify the tilt** — to say not just "they are related"
but *how strongly*, on a scale he could compare from one pair of traits to another. His
collaborator Karl Pearson sharpened Galton's "co-relation" into the formula we still use:
the **correlation coefficient**. The covariance is the un-normalized engine inside it.

Why was the existing toolkit inadequate? [PROB-06] gave us variance, a number for how much
*one* variable spreads. But variance is blind to a second variable. If you only ever
square deviations of $X$ by themselves, you can never detect that $X$'s deviations tend to
come paired with $Y$'s deviations. The fix is almost obvious in hindsight: instead of
multiplying each deviation of $X$ by *itself*, multiply it by the *simultaneous* deviation
of $Y$. That single change — from $(X-\mu_X)^2$ to $(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)$ — is the entire
conceptual leap of the article. Everything else is consequence.

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any integral, picture the data. Put $X$ on the horizontal axis and $Y$ on the
vertical, draw the mean lines $x=\mu_X$ and $y=\mu_Y$, and you have carved the plane into
four quadrants. Now look at where the points fall.

If $X$ and $Y$ rise together, points cluster in the **upper-right** (both above their
means) and **lower-left** (both below) quadrants. In the upper-right, $(X-\mu_X)>0$ and
$(Y-\mu_Y)>0$, so their **product is positive**. In the lower-left, both deviations are
negative, and a negative times a negative is again **positive**. So a cloud that tilts
up-and-to-the-right piles up positive products. If instead $Y$ falls as $X$ rises, points
land in the off-diagonal quadrants where one deviation is positive and the other negative,
so the products are **negative**. And if there is no tilt — points scattered evenly across
all four quadrants — the positive and negative products cancel and average out to roughly
**zero**.

That average signed product *is* the covariance. @fig-quadrants makes the picture concrete.

```{python}
#| label: fig-quadrants
#| fig-cap: "The sign of covariance, by quadrant. The mean lines $x=\\mu_X$ (vertical) and $y=\\mu_Y$ (horizontal) split the plane into four quadrants. In the green quadrants the product $(x-\\mu_X)(y-\\mu_Y)$ is **positive** (both deviations share a sign); in the red quadrants it is **negative**. **Left:** an up-tilted cloud lands mostly in the positive quadrants, so the average product — the covariance — is positive. **Right:** a down-tilted cloud lands in the negative quadrants, giving negative covariance. Covariance is just the average of the signed shaded-quadrant contributions."
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.2))

for ax, r, title in [(axL, 0.8, "Positive covariance"),
                     (axR, -0.8, "Negative covariance")]:
    mean = [0, 0]
    cov = [[1, r], [r, 1]]
    pts = rng.multivariate_normal(mean, cov, size=120)
    mx, my = pts[:, 0].mean(), pts[:, 1].mean()
    # shade quadrants
    lim = 3.2
    ax.axvspan(mx, lim, ymin=0.5, ymax=1, color=ACCENT, alpha=0.07)
    ax.axvspan(-lim, mx, ymin=0, ymax=0.5, color=ACCENT, alpha=0.07)
    ax.axvspan(mx, lim, ymin=0, ymax=0.5, color=LINE, alpha=0.07)
    ax.axvspan(-lim, mx, ymin=0.5, ymax=1, color=LINE, alpha=0.07)
    ax.axhline(my, color=GREY, lw=1.0)
    ax.axvline(mx, color=GREY, lw=1.0)
    ax.scatter(pts[:, 0], pts[:, 1], color=POINT, s=14, alpha=0.8)
    ax.set_xlim(-lim, lim); ax.set_ylim(-lim, lim)
    ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$")
    ax.set_title(title)
    ax.text(1.7, 1.7, "$+$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=16, ha="center")
    ax.text(-1.7, -1.7, "$+$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=16, ha="center")
    ax.text(1.7, -1.7, "$-$", color=LINE, fontsize=16, ha="center")
    ax.text(-1.7, 1.7, "$-$", color=LINE, fontsize=16, ha="center")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one sentence, what does covariance measure?"* The average, over the joint
distribution, of how far $X$ and $Y$ stray from their respective means **at the same
time, with matching sign**. Positive when they tend to be on the same side of their means
together, negative when on opposite sides, zero when there is no such tendency. The whole
formula is just that sentence written in symbols.
:::

# The joint distribution {#sec-joint}

Before covariance we need the object it averages over: the joint distribution. We treat
the discrete and continuous cases in parallel, exactly as [PROB-03] and [PROB-04] did for one
variable, because covariance will be defined identically for both.

## The discrete case: the joint pmf {#sec-joint-pmf}

::: {#def-joint-pmf}
**Definition (joint probability mass function).** For discrete random variables $X$ and
$Y$, the **joint pmf** is the function
$$
p_{X,Y}(x,y) = P(X=x \ \text{and}\ Y=y),
$$ {#eq-joint-pmf}
the probability that $X$ takes the value $x$ *and* $Y$ takes the value $y$ on the same
draw.
:::

Two properties follow directly from the probability axioms (built in [PROB-00]). First, each
value is a probability, so $p_{X,Y}(x,y)\ge 0$. Second, summing over **every** pair of
values must account for all the probability, because on any draw *some* pair $(x,y)$
occurs:
$$
\sum_{x}\sum_{y} p_{X,Y}(x,y) = 1.
$$ {#eq-joint-pmf-norm}
This is the two-dimensional version of [PROB-03]'s $\sum_x p(x)=1$; the only change is a
second summation index for the second variable.

A joint pmf over finitely many values is most honestly displayed as a **table**, one row
per value of $X$, one column per value of $Y$, with the probabilities in the cells. We will
build exactly such a table in @sec-example-rain.

## Recovering one variable: the marginal {#sec-marginals}

Here is the first real payoff of having the joint object. Suppose we have $p_{X,Y}$ but want
the distribution of $X$ *alone* — ignoring $Y$. The event "$X=x$" happens whenever $X=x$
paired with **any** value of $Y$. Those pairs are mutually exclusive (you cannot have
$Y=y_1$ and $Y=y_2$ on the same draw), so by countable additivity their probabilities add:

$$
p_X(x) = P(X=x) = P\!\left(\bigcup_{y}\{X=x,\,Y=y\}\right)
= \sum_{y} P(X=x,\,Y=y) = \sum_{y} p_{X,Y}(x,y).
$$ {#eq-marginal-pmf}

::: {#def-marginal-pmf}
**Definition (marginal pmf).** The **marginal pmf** of $X$ is obtained by summing the joint
pmf over all values of the other variable:
$$
p_X(x) = \sum_{y} p_{X,Y}(x,y), \qquad p_Y(y) = \sum_{x} p_{X,Y}(x,y).
$$
:::

The word *marginal* is wonderfully literal: write the joint pmf as a table and the
row-sums and column-sums land in the **margins** of the page. The marginal of $X$ is the
column of row-totals; the marginal of $Y$ is the row of column-totals. We are simply
collapsing one dimension by adding it up.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Can you reconstruct the joint distribution from the two marginals?"* **No — and this is
the crux of why the joint object exists.** The marginals are sums of the joint table; many
different tables can produce the same row-sums and column-sums. Reconstructing the joint
from the marginals would be like recovering a matrix from only its row and column totals —
hopeless without more information. The one exception is **independence**, where
$p_{X,Y}(x,y)=p_X(x)\,p_Y(y)$ exactly; that special factoring is the subject of [PROB-08].
The general inadequacy of marginals is precisely the gap covariance was invented to start
filling.
:::

## The continuous case: the joint pdf {#sec-joint-pdf}

When $X$ and $Y$ are continuous, probability is spread over the plane as a *density*, the
direct analogue of the single-variable pdf from [PROB-04].

::: {#def-joint-pdf}
**Definition (joint probability density function).** A function $f_{X,Y}(x,y)\ge 0$ is the
**joint pdf** of $(X,Y)$ if, for every region $A$ of the plane,
$$
P\big((X,Y)\in A\big) = \iint_{A} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\,dy,
$$ {#eq-joint-pdf}
and over the whole plane it integrates to $1$:
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\,dy = 1.
$$ {#eq-joint-pdf-norm}
:::

::: {.callout-note title="The one above-floor ingredient, flagged"}
@eq-joint-pdf uses a **double integral** — integrating over a two-dimensional region.
Double integrals sit just above our Calculus-I floor, so we say plainly what we are using
and nothing more: a double integral $\iint_A f\,dx\,dy$ is computed as an **iterated
integral**, integrating first over one variable (treating the other as a constant, exactly
the Calc-1 single-variable integral) and then over the second. Concretely,
$\iint_A f\,dx\,dy = \int\big(\int f\,dy\big)dx$. Every double integral in this article is
evaluated by this "integrate one variable at a time" rule and nothing fancier; the full
machinery of multivariable calculus (changes of variables with Jacobians, Fubini's theorem)
is built where it is owned, in [PROB-18] and the Geometry-of-Data track. Where we need
*why* the order of integration may be swapped, we will only ever do it for non-negative
densities, where it is safe.
:::

As with one variable, the density is **not** a probability — $P(X=x,\,Y=y)=0$ for any
single point, since a point has zero area. Density becomes probability only after
integrating over a region of positive area. The marginal works the same way as the
discrete case, with integration replacing summation: to forget $Y$, integrate it out.

::: {#def-marginal-pdf}
**Definition (marginal pdf).** The **marginal pdf** of $X$ is
$$
f_X(x) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy,
\qquad
f_Y(y) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx.
$$ {#eq-marginal-pdf}
:::

To see why integrating out $Y$ gives the density of $X$, fix a small interval
$[x, x+dx]$. The probability that $X$ lands in it, regardless of $Y$, is the integral of
the joint density over the infinite vertical strip above that interval:
$P(x\le X\le x+dx)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\big(\int_x^{x+dx} f_{X,Y}\,dx'\big)dy \approx
\big(\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy\big)\,dx$. Matching this against the
single-variable defining relation $P(x\le X\le x+dx)\approx f_X(x)\,dx$ from [PROB-04]
identifies $f_X(x)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dy$, which is @eq-marginal-pdf.

## The joint CDF: the one object that works for both {#sec-joint-cdf}

Just as the single-variable CDF $F(x)=P(X\le x)$ described discrete, continuous, and mixed
variables uniformly in [PROB-02], a **joint CDF** does the same for pairs.

::: {#def-joint-cdf}
**Definition (joint CDF).** The **joint cumulative distribution function** of $(X,Y)$ is
$$
F_{X,Y}(x,y) = P(X\le x \ \text{and}\ Y\le y),
$$ {#eq-joint-cdf}
the probability that *both* coordinates fall at or below the given thresholds — the total
probability mass in the lower-left quadrant anchored at $(x,y)$.
:::

In the continuous case the joint CDF is the running double integral of the density,
$F_{X,Y}(x,y)=\int_{-\infty}^{x}\int_{-\infty}^{y} f_{X,Y}(s,t)\,dt\,ds$, and conversely the
density is recovered by differentiating once in each variable,
$f_{X,Y}=\partial^2 F_{X,Y}/\partial x\,\partial y$ — the two-dimensional shadow of [PROB-04]'s
"the pdf is the derivative of the CDF." We will not lean on the joint CDF much in this
article (covariance is computed from the pmf/pdf via expectation), but it is the object
that makes "joint distribution" precise for *any* pair of random variables, and it is what
the empirical version in [PROB-22] estimates.

# Expectation of a function of two variables {#sec-expectation-2d}

Covariance is an expectation, so we first need the expectation of a function $g(X,Y)$ of
*both* variables. This is the two-variable **law of the unconscious statistician**, and it
extends [PROB-05]'s one-variable rule in the only way it could.

::: {#thm-lotus2}
**Theorem (LOTUS, two variables).** For a function $g$ of two random variables,
$$
E[g(X,Y)] = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} g(x,y)\,p_{X,Y}(x,y) \quad\text{(discrete)},
\qquad
E[g(X,Y)] = \iint g(x,y)\,f_{X,Y}(x,y)\,dx\,dy \quad\text{(continuous)}.
$$
:::

The principle is identical to one variable: to average $g(X,Y)$, weight each possible value
$g(x,y)$ by how probable the input pair $(x,y)$ is, then add (or integrate). We do not need
the distribution of the *output* $g(X,Y)$ — hence "unconscious." Because this rule is the
sole computational engine of the rest of the article — every covariance below is an instance
of it — we prove the discrete case in full rather than wave at it.

::: {.proof}
Write $W=g(X,Y)$ for the output random variable, and let $w$ range over the distinct values
$W$ can take. By the [PROB-05] definition of expectation applied to $W$ directly,
$$
E[W] = \sum_{w} w\,P(W=w).
$$
This is the "conscious" computation: it needs the distribution of the output $W$, which is
exactly what we want to avoid finding. To eliminate it, expand the event $\{W=w\}$ into the
input pairs that produce it. The pairs $(x,y)$ with $g(x,y)=w$ are mutually exclusive (a
single draw lands on one pair), so by the countable additivity of [PROB-00],
$$
P(W=w) = \sum_{(x,y)\,:\,g(x,y)=w} p_{X,Y}(x,y).
$$
Substitute this into the sum and, inside the inner sum, replace the constant $w$ by $g(x,y)$
— legal precisely *because* every pair in that inner sum satisfies $g(x,y)=w$:
$$
E[W] = \sum_{w} w\!\!\sum_{(x,y)\,:\,g(x,y)=w}\!\! p_{X,Y}(x,y)
     = \sum_{w}\ \sum_{(x,y)\,:\,g(x,y)=w}\!\! g(x,y)\,p_{X,Y}(x,y).
$$
The outer sum over $w$ together with the inner sum over the pairs mapping to that $w$
visits **every** pair $(x,y)$ in the support exactly once — the values of $g$ partition the
pairs into groups, and we are summing over every group and then over every member. So the
double sum collapses to a single unrestricted sum over all pairs:
$$
E[g(X,Y)] = \sum_{x}\sum_{y} g(x,y)\,p_{X,Y}(x,y),
$$
which is the theorem. The continuous statement is the identical argument with the partition
into level sets handled by integration in place of the countable sum (the measure-theoretic
bookkeeping that makes "partition by the value of $g$" rigorous for densities is owned by
[PROB-04]'s machinery); the formula it yields is the displayed integral.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

The only change from the one-variable LOTUS of [PROB-05] is the second summation/integration
index — the grouping-by-output argument is word-for-word the same.

One immediate, load-bearing consequence: **linearity of expectation extends to several
variables.** Taking $g(X,Y)=aX+bY$ for constants $a,b$,
$$
E[aX+bY] = \iint (ax+by)\,f_{X,Y}\,dx\,dy
= a\iint x\,f_{X,Y}\,dx\,dy + b\iint y\,f_{X,Y}\,dx\,dy.
$$
In the first integral, integrate out $y$ first: $\int f_{X,Y}\,dy = f_X(x)$ by
@eq-marginal-pdf, leaving $a\int x f_X(x)\,dx = aE[X]$. The second integral likewise gives
$bE[Y]$. Therefore
$$
\boxed{\,E[aX+bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y].\,}
$$ {#eq-linearity}
This held in [PROB-05] for functions of *one* variable; we have just confirmed it survives
intact for a linear combination of *two*, and crucially it required **no assumption that
$X$ and $Y$ are independent**. We will lean on @eq-linearity constantly.

# Covariance {#sec-covariance}

We can now define and develop the central object.

::: {#def-covariance}
**Definition (covariance).** Let $X$ and $Y$ have means $\mu_X=E[X]$ and $\mu_Y=E[Y]$. The
**covariance** of $X$ and $Y$ is
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = E\big[(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)\big].
$$ {#eq-cov-def}
:::

Hold @eq-cov-def against the definition of variance from [PROB-06],
$\operatorname{Var}(X)=E[(X-\mu_X)^2]=E[(X-\mu_X)(X-\mu_X)]$. They are the **same formula**;
covariance simply pairs $X$'s deviation with $Y$'s instead of with its own. This is the
single most useful fact to carry: **variance is covariance of a variable with itself.**

$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X,X) = E\big[(X-\mu_X)(X-\mu_X)\big] = E\big[(X-\mu_X)^2\big]
= \operatorname{Var}(X).
$$ {#eq-cov-is-var}

Everything we proved about variance in [PROB-06] is therefore a special case of what we are
about to prove about covariance.

## The computational identity {#sec-cov-shortcut}

Computing covariance straight from @eq-cov-def means first finding both means, then
forming deviations. There is a shortcut that mirrors [PROB-06]'s
$\operatorname{Var}(X)=E[X^2]-(E[X])^2$, and we derive it with every step shown. Start by
expanding the product inside the expectation with ordinary FOIL:
$$
(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y) = XY - X\mu_Y - \mu_X Y + \mu_X\mu_Y.
$$
Now take the expectation of both sides and use linearity (@eq-linearity, extended to four
terms — which is just applying the two-term rule three times). Crucially, $\mu_X$ and
$\mu_Y$ are **constants** (they are the fixed numbers $E[X]$ and $E[Y]$), so they pull
straight out of any expectation, and $E[\text{constant}]$ is that constant:
$$
E[(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)]
= E[XY] - \mu_Y\,E[X] - \mu_X\,E[Y] + \mu_X\mu_Y.
$$
Substitute $E[X]=\mu_X$ and $E[Y]=\mu_Y$ into the middle two terms:
$$
= E[XY] - \mu_Y\mu_X - \mu_X\mu_Y + \mu_X\mu_Y = E[XY] - \mu_X\mu_Y.
$$
The last two of the three $\mu_X\mu_Y$ terms cancel against each other, leaving exactly one.
Therefore

$$
\boxed{\,\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]\,E[Y].\,}
$$ {#eq-cov-shortcut}

Read it: covariance is the **average of the product minus the product of the averages**.
Setting $Y=X$ recovers $\operatorname{Var}(X)=E[X^2]-(E[X])^2$ exactly, confirming the
shortcut is the honest generalization of the [PROB-06] one. We will use @eq-cov-shortcut for
every numerical example because it needs only three ordinary expectations: $E[XY]$, $E[X]$,
$E[Y]$.

## Bilinearity: covariance is linear in each slot {#sec-bilinearity}

Covariance's most powerful algebraic property is that it behaves like multiplication: it is
**linear in each argument separately**, a property called **bilinearity**. We state it,
then prove every piece.

::: {#thm-bilinearity}
**Theorem (properties of covariance).** For random variables $X,Y,Z$ and constants $a,b,c$:

1. **(Symmetry)** $\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=\operatorname{Cov}(Y,X)$.
2. **(Constants drop out)** $\operatorname{Cov}(X,c)=0$.
3. **(Scaling)** $\operatorname{Cov}(aX, bY)=ab\,\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)$.
4. **(Additivity)** $\operatorname{Cov}(X+Z,\,Y)=\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)+\operatorname{Cov}(Z,Y)$.
5. **(Shift invariance)** $\operatorname{Cov}(X+a,\,Y+b)=\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)$.
:::

::: {.proof}
We prove each from @eq-cov-shortcut and linearity of expectation; none needs independence.

**(1) Symmetry.** Multiplication of numbers commutes, so $XY=YX$, hence
$E[XY]=E[YX]$ and $E[X]E[Y]=E[Y]E[X]$. Therefore
$\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=E[XY]-E[X]E[Y]=E[YX]-E[Y]E[X]=\operatorname{Cov}(Y,X)$.

**(2) Constants drop out.** Let $c$ be a constant, viewed as a (degenerate) random
variable that always equals $c$. Then $E[c]=c$ and $E[Xc]=E[cX]=cE[X]$ by linearity. So
$\operatorname{Cov}(X,c)=E[Xc]-E[X]E[c]=cE[X]-E[X]\cdot c = 0$. A quantity that never
varies cannot co-vary with anything.

**(3) Scaling.** Using @eq-cov-shortcut and pulling the constants $a,b$ through every
expectation by linearity,
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(aX,bY)=E[(aX)(bY)] - E[aX]\,E[bY]
= ab\,E[XY] - (aE[X])(bE[Y])
= ab\big(E[XY]-E[X]E[Y]\big) = ab\,\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y).
$$

**(4) Additivity.** Again by @eq-cov-shortcut and linearity, expanding the product
$(X+Z)Y=XY+ZY$:
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X+Z,\,Y) = E[(X+Z)Y] - E[X+Z]\,E[Y]
= \big(E[XY]+E[ZY]\big) - \big(E[X]+E[Z]\big)E[Y].
$$
Distribute the $E[Y]$ and regroup the terms into two covariances:
$$
= \underbrace{\big(E[XY]-E[X]E[Y]\big)}_{\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)}
+ \underbrace{\big(E[ZY]-E[Z]E[Y]\big)}_{\operatorname{Cov}(Z,Y)}.
$$

**(5) Shift invariance.** Adding a constant $a$ shifts $X$ but not its deviations from its
mean: if $W=X+a$ then $\mu_W=E[X+a]=\mu_X+a$ by linearity, so
$W-\mu_W = (X+a)-(\mu_X+a)=X-\mu_X$ — the $+a$ cancels. The same holds for $Y+b$. Since
covariance @eq-cov-def depends only on the deviations, it is unchanged:
$\operatorname{Cov}(X+a,Y+b)=E[(X-\mu_X)(Y-\mu_Y)]=\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Properties (3) and (4) together are what "bilinear" means, and combining them gives the
fully general rule we will use again and again — covariance distributes over linear
combinations exactly like a product distributes over sums:
$$
\operatorname{Cov}\!\Big(\sum_i a_i X_i,\ \sum_j b_j Y_j\Big)
= \sum_i\sum_j a_i b_j\,\operatorname{Cov}(X_i, Y_j).
$$ {#eq-cov-bilinear-general}

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why does adding a constant change the mean but not the covariance?"* Because covariance
lives entirely in the *deviations from the mean*, and shifting a variable shifts its mean
by the same amount — so the deviations, the gaps to the mean, are untouched (property 5).
The interviewer is checking that you understand covariance measures **co-movement**, which
is about *changes*, not *levels*. This is also why you may center your data (subtract the
means) before computing covariance without changing a thing — exactly the centering trick
Regression uses.
:::

# The variance of a sum {#sec-var-sum}

We can now derive the identity the Core promised — the one that powers portfolio risk,
the variance of sample means, and the whole sums-of-squares story in regression. It falls
straight out of bilinearity plus @eq-cov-is-var.

::: {#thm-var-sum}
**Theorem (variance of a sum).** For any two random variables $X$ and $Y$,
$$
\operatorname{Var}(X+Y) = \operatorname{Var}(X) + \operatorname{Var}(Y)
+ 2\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y).
$$ {#eq-var-sum}
:::

::: {.proof}
By @eq-cov-is-var, a variance is a self-covariance, so $\operatorname{Var}(X+Y)
=\operatorname{Cov}(X+Y,\,X+Y)$. Now expand using bilinearity — additivity in the first
slot (property 4), then again in the second slot:
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X+Y,\,X+Y)
= \operatorname{Cov}(X,X) + \operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) + \operatorname{Cov}(Y,X) + \operatorname{Cov}(Y,Y).
$$
The four terms are the entries of a $2\times2$ "multiplication table" of the sum with
itself. By @eq-cov-is-var the corner terms are variances:
$\operatorname{Cov}(X,X)=\operatorname{Var}(X)$ and
$\operatorname{Cov}(Y,Y)=\operatorname{Var}(Y)$. By symmetry (property 1) the two cross
terms are equal: $\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=\operatorname{Cov}(Y,X)$, so together they make
$2\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)$. Collecting,
$$
\operatorname{Var}(X+Y) = \operatorname{Var}(X) + \operatorname{Var}(Y) + 2\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y).
\qquad\blacksquare
$$
:::

The structure is exactly the algebraic identity $(a+b)^2=a^2+b^2+2ab$, with variances
playing the role of squares and the covariance playing the role of the cross-term $2ab$.
That is not a coincidence; it is the same bilinear expansion. Three consequences are worth
stating outright.

**The difference.** Replacing $Y$ by $-Y$ and using scaling (property 3,
$\operatorname{Cov}(X,-Y)=-\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)$ and
$\operatorname{Var}(-Y)=(-1)^2\operatorname{Var}(Y)=\operatorname{Var}(Y)$):
$$
\operatorname{Var}(X-Y) = \operatorname{Var}(X) + \operatorname{Var}(Y) - 2\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y).
$$ {#eq-var-diff}
Note the variance of a *difference* still *adds* the two variances — it is the cross-term
whose sign flips. A common error is to expect $\operatorname{Var}(X-Y)=\operatorname{Var}(X)-\operatorname{Var}(Y)$;
the theorem shows that is wrong unless the covariance happens to equal $\operatorname{Var}(Y)$.

**The uncorrelated case.** If $\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=0$, the cross-term vanishes and
$$
\operatorname{Var}(X+Y)=\operatorname{Var}(X)+\operatorname{Var}(Y),
$$
"variances add." This is the seed of why the sample mean's variance shrinks like $1/n$ — a
result [PROB-08] develops in full for i.i.d. sums and that powers every confidence interval
downstream.

**The general sum of $n$ terms.** Applying @eq-cov-bilinear-general to
$\operatorname{Var}(\sum_i X_i)=\operatorname{Cov}(\sum_i X_i,\sum_j X_j)$ gives
$$
\operatorname{Var}\!\Big(\sum_{i=1}^{n} X_i\Big)
= \sum_{i=1}^{n}\operatorname{Var}(X_i) + 2\!\!\sum_{1\le i<j\le n}\!\!\operatorname{Cov}(X_i, X_j),
$$ {#eq-var-sum-n}
where the diagonal terms ($i=j$) are the variances and the off-diagonal terms pair up by
symmetry into the factor of 2. This is the form [PROB-17] repackages as
$\operatorname{Var}(\mathbf{a}^\top X)=\mathbf{a}^\top\Sigma\,\mathbf{a}$ once the
covariances are collected into a matrix $\Sigma$.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"A portfolio puts weight $w$ in asset A and $1-w$ in asset B. Write its variance."* This
is @eq-var-sum with scaling. The return is $R = wA + (1-w)B$, so
$$
\operatorname{Var}(R) = w^2\operatorname{Var}(A) + (1-w)^2\operatorname{Var}(B)
+ 2w(1-w)\operatorname{Cov}(A,B).
$$
The whole theory of diversification is in the last term: if $\operatorname{Cov}(A,B)$ is
negative, the cross-term *subtracts*, and the portfolio is **less** risky than either asset
alone. Combining two risky assets to get a less risky whole is the covariance term doing
visible work — the single most cited application of @eq-var-sum. We compute a real instance
in @sec-example-portfolio.
:::

# Correlation: covariance with the units removed {#sec-correlation}

Covariance has a fatal practical flaw: **its size is uninterpretable.** If $X$ is measured
in dollars and $Y$ in years, the covariance carries units of dollar-years, and its
numerical value scales with whatever units you chose — switch dollars to cents and the
covariance multiplies by 100 (property 3 with $a=100$), though nothing about the
relationship changed. A covariance of "5,000,000" tells you nothing until you know the
scales. We need to **strip the units off** to get a pure, comparable number.

The natural denominator is suggested by property 3: dividing by a standard deviation of
each variable cancels its scale exactly. Define:

::: {#def-correlation}
**Definition (Pearson correlation coefficient).** For random variables with
$\operatorname{Var}(X)>0$ and $\operatorname{Var}(Y)>0$, the **correlation** is
$$
\rho_{X,Y} = \frac{\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sigma_X\,\sigma_Y}
= \frac{\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)}{\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(X)}\,\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(Y)}},
$$ {#eq-corr-def}
where $\sigma_X=\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(X)}$ and $\sigma_Y=\sqrt{\operatorname{Var}(Y)}$ are
the standard deviations from [PROB-06].
:::

First, confirm the units cancel and the scale-dependence dies. Under any rescaling
$X\mapsto aX$, $Y\mapsto bY$ (with $a,b>0$), the numerator scales by $ab$ (property 3) and
the denominator scales by $\sigma_{aX}\sigma_{bY}=|a|\sigma_X\cdot|b|\sigma_Y=ab\,\sigma_X\sigma_Y$.
The $ab$ cancels top and bottom:
$$
\rho_{aX,bY} = \frac{ab\,\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)}{ab\,\sigma_X\sigma_Y} = \rho_{X,Y}.
$$
Correlation is **dimensionless and scale-free** — exactly the comparable number Galton and
Pearson needed.

## Correlation lives in $[-1,1]$ {#sec-corr-bound}

The defining property of correlation is its bound, and it has a clean proof that uses
nothing heavier than "a variance is never negative" (from [PROB-06], since variance is an
average of squares).

::: {#thm-corr-bound}
**Theorem (correlation bound).** For any $X,Y$ with positive variances,
$$
-1 \le \rho_{X,Y} \le 1.
$$
Moreover $\rho_{X,Y}=\pm 1$ if and only if $Y$ is an exact linear function of $X$, i.e.
$Y = a + bX$ for constants $b\ne 0$ (with $a$ arbitrary), and the sign of $\rho$ matches
the sign of $b$.
:::

::: {.proof}
**Step 1 — standardize.** Let $X^* = (X-\mu_X)/\sigma_X$ and $Y^*=(Y-\mu_Y)/\sigma_Y$ be the
standardized variables (the z-scores from [PROB-06]). Each has mean $0$ and variance $1$: by
shift- and scale-rules, $E[X^*]=0$ and
$\operatorname{Var}(X^*)=\operatorname{Var}(X)/\sigma_X^2=1$, likewise for $Y^*$. Their
covariance is the correlation itself, because by bilinearity (properties 3 and 5),
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X^*,Y^*) = \operatorname{Cov}\!\Big(\tfrac{X-\mu_X}{\sigma_X},\tfrac{Y-\mu_Y}{\sigma_Y}\Big)
= \frac{1}{\sigma_X\sigma_Y}\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = \rho_{X,Y}.
$$
And since $X^*,Y^*$ have unit variance, $E[(X^*)^2]=\operatorname{Var}(X^*)=1$ and
$E[(Y^*)^2]=1$ (their means are zero, so $E[\cdot^2]=\operatorname{Var}$).

**Step 2 — the non-negativity squeeze.** Consider the variable $X^* + Y^*$. A variance is
the expectation of a square, hence $\ge 0$:
$$
0 \le \operatorname{Var}(X^*+Y^*) = \operatorname{Var}(X^*) + \operatorname{Var}(Y^*) + 2\operatorname{Cov}(X^*,Y^*)
= 1 + 1 + 2\rho = 2 + 2\rho,
$$
using @eq-var-sum in the middle. From $0\le 2+2\rho$ we get $\rho \ge -1$.

Now do the same with the *difference* $X^*-Y^*$, using @eq-var-diff:
$$
0 \le \operatorname{Var}(X^*-Y^*) = 1 + 1 - 2\rho = 2 - 2\rho,
$$
which gives $\rho \le 1$. Together: $-1\le\rho\le 1$.

**Step 3 — the equality cases.** Equality $\rho=1$ forces $\operatorname{Var}(X^*-Y^*)=0$.
A random variable with zero variance is **constant** with probability one (its deviations
from its mean are zero on average *and* squared, so they are zero), so $X^*-Y^*=c$ for some
constant $c$; taking expectations, $c=E[X^*]-E[Y^*]=0$, hence $X^*=Y^*$. Writing this back
in original units, $(X-\mu_X)/\sigma_X=(Y-\mu_Y)/\sigma_Y$, which rearranges to
$Y = \mu_Y + \frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_X}(X-\mu_X)$ — an exact line with **positive** slope
$\sigma_Y/\sigma_X>0$. Symmetrically, $\rho=-1$ forces $\operatorname{Var}(X^*+Y^*)=0$,
giving $X^*=-Y^*$ and an exact line with **negative** slope $-\sigma_Y/\sigma_X$. The
converse — that an exact line $Y=a+bX$ gives $\rho=\operatorname{sign}(b)$ — follows by
plugging $Y=a+bX$ into @eq-corr-def: $\operatorname{Cov}(X,a+bX)=b\operatorname{Var}(X)$,
$\sigma_{a+bX}=|b|\sigma_X$, so $\rho = b\operatorname{Var}(X)/(\sigma_X\cdot|b|\sigma_X)
= b/|b| = \operatorname{sign}(b)$.
$\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

This proof is worth absorbing because it is the probabilistic shadow of the
**Cauchy–Schwarz inequality** — the statement that
$|\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)|\le\sigma_X\sigma_Y$ — proved here using only that variances are
non-negative. The geometric reading (covariance as an inner product, correlation as the
cosine of an angle between centered variables) is exactly how the Geometry-of-Data track
sees it; we have derived the inequality without needing that machinery.

@fig-corr-grid shows what a range of correlations actually looks like as point clouds.

```{python}
#| label: fig-corr-grid
#| fig-cap: "What correlation looks like. Each panel is a sample from a bivariate normal with the labeled correlation $\\rho$. At $\\rho=0$ the cloud is round (no linear tilt); as $|\\rho|\\to 1$ it collapses toward a line, positively sloped for $\\rho>0$ and negatively for $\\rho<0$. At $\\rho=\\pm 1$ (not shown) the points would lie exactly on a line. Correlation measures *only* this tilt-toward-a-line — a fact @fig-zero-cov will exploit."
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
rhos = [-0.9, -0.5, 0.0, 0.4, 0.8, 0.99]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(9.0, 6.0))
for ax, r in zip(axes.ravel(), rhos):
    cov = [[1, r], [r, 1]]
    pts = rng.multivariate_normal([0, 0], cov, size=300)
    ax.scatter(pts[:, 0], pts[:, 1], color=POINT, s=9, alpha=0.6)
    ax.set_title(fr"$\rho = {r}$", fontsize=11)
    ax.set_xlim(-3.5, 3.5); ax.set_ylim(-3.5, 3.5)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What is the relationship between correlation and the slope of a regression line?"* If you
regress $Y$ on $X$ by least squares (the subject of [REGR-01]), the slope is
$\hat\beta_1 = \operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)/\operatorname{Var}(X)$. To connect it to
$\rho=\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)/(\sigma_X\sigma_Y)$, solve the latter for the covariance,
$\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=\rho\,\sigma_X\sigma_Y$, and substitute into the slope, using
$\operatorname{Var}(X)=\sigma_X^2$:
$$
\hat\beta_1 = \frac{\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)}{\operatorname{Var}(X)}
= \frac{\rho\,\sigma_X\sigma_Y}{\sigma_X^2}
= \rho\,\frac{\sigma_Y}{\sigma_X}.
$$
So the slope is the correlation rescaled by the
ratio of spreads; if you first standardize both variables to unit variance, *the slope
equals the correlation*. Correlation is the **scale-free** cousin of the regression slope —
the same co-movement, one with units, one without.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We run the machinery on honest data, including one example at the edge where covariance
gives a confident, completely misleading answer of zero.

## Example 1 — rain and umbrellas: a joint table by hand {#sec-example-rain}

We return to the motivating pair. Over a year of records for one city, suppose the joint
pmf of $X=\mathbf{1}\{\text{rain}\}$ and $Y=\mathbf{1}\{\text{umbrella carried}\}$ (each
$0$ or $1$) is the following table — every cell a probability, all four summing to $1$.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-rain
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
# Joint pmf p(x,y): rows X in {0,1} (no rain / rain), cols Y in {0,1} (no umb / umb)
p = np.array([
    [0.55, 0.15],   # X=0 (no rain):  P(no umb), P(umb)
    [0.05, 0.25],   # X=1 (rain):     P(no umb), P(umb)
])
xv = np.array([0, 1]); yv = np.array([0, 1])
pX = p.sum(axis=1)              # marginal of X (row sums)
pY = p.sum(axis=0)              # marginal of Y (col sums)
EX = (xv * pX).sum()
EY = (yv * pY).sum()
EXY = sum(xv[i]*yv[j]*p[i, j] for i in range(2) for j in range(2))
covXY = EXY - EX*EY
VarX = (xv**2 * pX).sum() - EX**2
VarY = (yv**2 * pY).sum() - EY**2
rho = covXY / np.sqrt(VarX*VarY)
print("Joint pmf p(x,y):")
print("                 Y=0(no umb)   Y=1(umbrella)   row sum p_X(x)")
print(f"  X=0 (no rain):   {p[0,0]:.2f}          {p[0,1]:.2f}            {pX[0]:.2f}")
print(f"  X=1 (rain):      {p[1,0]:.2f}          {p[1,1]:.2f}            {pX[1]:.2f}")
print(f"  col sum p_Y(y):  {pY[0]:.2f}          {pY[1]:.2f}            {p.sum():.2f}")
print()
print(f"E[X]  = {EX:.3f}     E[Y]  = {EY:.3f}     E[XY] = {EXY:.3f}")
print(f"Cov(X,Y) = E[XY]-E[X]E[Y] = {EXY:.3f} - ({EX:.3f})({EY:.3f}) = {covXY:.4f}")
print(f"Var(X) = {VarX:.4f}   Var(Y) = {VarY:.4f}")
print(f"rho = Cov/(sX sY) = {covXY:.4f} / sqrt({VarX:.4f}*{VarY:.4f}) = {rho:.4f}")
```

Walk the arithmetic the code printed, plugging straight into the boxed formulas.

- **Marginals.** Summing each row gives $p_X(0)=`{python} f"{pX[0]:.2f}"`$ (no rain) and
  $p_X(1)=`{python} f"{pX[1]:.2f}"`$ (rain): it rains 40% of days. Summing each column gives
  $p_Y(1)=`{python} f"{pY[1]:.2f}"`$ — an umbrella is carried 40% of days. These are the
  *margins* of the table, @sec-marginals made literal.
- **Means.** For a $0/1$ (indicator) variable, $E[X]=0\cdot p_X(0)+1\cdot p_X(1)=p_X(1)=`{python} f"{EX:.2f}"`$,
  and likewise $E[Y]=`{python} f"{EY:.2f}"`$. The mean of an indicator is just the
  probability of the event — a fact [PROB-11] leans on heavily.
- **The product term.** By LOTUS (@thm-lotus2), $E[XY]=\sum_{x,y}xy\,p(x,y)$. The product
  $xy$ is $1$ only in the single cell $X=1,Y=1$ and $0$ everywhere else, so
  $E[XY]=p(1,1)=`{python} f"{p[1,1]:.2f}"`$.
- **Covariance.** By @eq-cov-shortcut,
  $\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)=E[XY]-E[X]E[Y]=`{python} f"{EXY:.2f}"`-(`{python} f"{EX:.2f}"`)(`{python} f"{EY:.2f}"`)=`{python} f"{covXY:.3f}"`$.
  Positive, as it must be: umbrellas come out *when* it rains.
- **Correlation.** Each variance is $p(1-p)$ for an indicator (the [PROB-06]/[PROB-11]
  result), and dividing the covariance by $\sigma_X\sigma_Y$ gives
  $\rho=`{python} f"{rho:.3f}"`$ — a strong but imperfect positive correlation, sensibly
  short of 1 because the link is real but not deterministic.

This is the entire pipeline of the article on a $2\times2$ table: joint pmf $\to$ marginals
$\to$ means $\to$ covariance $\to$ correlation, with no step hidden.

## Example 2 — a continuous joint density {#sec-example-continuous}

Now the continuous machinery. Let $(X,Y)$ have joint density on the unit square
$$
f_{X,Y}(x,y) = x + y, \qquad 0\le x\le 1,\ 0\le y\le 1,
$$
and zero elsewhere. (Quick legitimacy check via @eq-joint-pdf-norm:
$\int_0^1\!\int_0^1 (x+y)\,dx\,dy = \int_0^1(\tfrac12 + y)\,dy = \tfrac12+\tfrac12 = 1$, so
it is a valid density.) We compute the covariance by hand, integrating one variable at a
time as flagged in @sec-joint-pdf.

**Marginal of $X$** (integrate out $y$, @eq-marginal-pdf), for $0\le x\le 1$:
$$
f_X(x) = \int_0^1 (x+y)\,dy = \Big[xy + \tfrac{y^2}{2}\Big]_{y=0}^{1} = x + \tfrac12.
$$
The density is unchanged when we swap the names $x$ and $y$ (since $x+y=y+x$), so the same
integral with the roles reversed gives $f_Y(y)=\int_0^1(x+y)\,dx = y+\tfrac12$.

**Mean of $X$** (a [PROB-05] integral against the marginal):
$$
E[X] = \int_0^1 x\big(x+\tfrac12\big)\,dx = \int_0^1\big(x^2 + \tfrac{x}{2}\big)\,dx
= \tfrac13 + \tfrac14 = \tfrac{7}{12}.
$$
Because $f_Y$ has the identical form $y+\tfrac12$, the same integral gives
$E[Y]=\int_0^1 y(y+\tfrac12)\,dy = \tfrac13+\tfrac14 = \tfrac{7}{12}$.

**The product term** $E[XY]$ (LOTUS, @thm-lotus2, integrating $x$ first):
$$
E[XY] = \int_0^1\!\!\int_0^1 xy\,(x+y)\,dx\,dy
= \int_0^1\!\!\int_0^1 \big(x^2 y + x y^2\big)\,dx\,dy.
$$
Integrate over $x$ (treating $y$ as constant):
$$
\int_0^1 \big(x^2 y + x y^2\big)\,dx = \Big[\tfrac{x^3}{3}y + \tfrac{x^2}{2}y^2\Big]_{0}^{1}
= \tfrac{y}{3} + \tfrac{y^2}{2}.
$$
Then over $y$:
$$
E[XY] = \int_0^1\Big(\tfrac{y}{3} + \tfrac{y^2}{2}\Big)\,dy = \tfrac{1}{6} + \tfrac{1}{6} = \tfrac13.
$$

**Covariance** by @eq-cov-shortcut:
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] = \tfrac13 - \Big(\tfrac{7}{12}\Big)^2
= \tfrac13 - \tfrac{49}{144} = \tfrac{48}{144} - \tfrac{49}{144} = -\tfrac{1}{144}.
$$

The covariance is **negative** — a small but genuine tendency for large $X$ to come with
small $Y$. The verification figure below confirms the by-hand value against a large
Monte-Carlo sample, and shows the density surface.

```{python}
#| label: fig-continuous
#| fig-cap: "The joint density $f(x,y)=x+y$ on the unit square. **Left:** the density as a heatmap — it tilts upward toward the $(1,1)$ corner where both variables are large. **Right:** a scatter of $40{,}000$ points drawn from it (via rejection sampling). The slight negative covariance is invisible to the eye at this scale, which is the honest point: $\\rho=-1/144 \\div (\\sigma_X\\sigma_Y)$ is tiny. The hand-computed $\\operatorname{Cov}=-1/144\\approx-0.00694$ matches the sample covariance printed below."
# exact pieces
EX_c = 7/12; EXY_c = 1/3
cov_c = EXY_c - EX_c**2
# Var(X) = E[X^2] - E[X]^2 ;  E[X^2] = int x^2 (x+1/2) dx = 1/4 + 1/6 = 5/12
EX2_c = 1/4 + 1/6
Var_c = EX2_c - EX_c**2
rho_c = cov_c / Var_c   # sigma_X = sigma_Y by symmetry

# rejection sampling from f(x,y)=x+y on [0,1]^2 (max density = 2)
rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
N = 200000
xx = rng.uniform(0, 1, N); yy = rng.uniform(0, 1, N); uu = rng.uniform(0, 2, N)
keep = uu < (xx + yy)
xs, ys = xx[keep][:40000], yy[keep][:40000]
samp_cov = np.cov(xs, ys)[0, 1]

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 4.0))
gx = np.linspace(0, 1, 200); gy = np.linspace(0, 1, 200)
GX, GY = np.meshgrid(gx, gy)
im = axL.imshow(GX + GY, origin="lower", extent=[0, 1, 0, 1], cmap="viridis", aspect="auto")
axL.set_title("density $f(x,y)=x+y$"); axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("$y$")
fig.colorbar(im, ax=axL, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)
axL.grid(False)
axR.scatter(xs, ys, s=2, color=POINT, alpha=0.15)
axR.set_title("40,000 samples"); axR.set_xlabel("$x$"); axR.set_ylabel("$y$")
axR.set_xlim(0, 1); axR.set_ylim(0, 1); axR.grid(False)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"hand  Cov(X,Y) = -1/144 = {cov_c:.6f}")
print(f"MC    Cov(X,Y)         = {samp_cov:.6f}")
print(f"hand  Var(X)   = 11/144 = {Var_c:.6f}")
print(f"hand  rho      = {rho_c:.6f}")
```

## Example 3 — covariance at work: portfolio variance {#sec-example-portfolio}

A direct, realistic use of @eq-var-sum. Two assets have daily-return standard deviations
$\sigma_A=1.2\%$ and $\sigma_B=0.9\%$. We sweep the correlation $\rho_{AB}$ from $-1$ to
$+1$ and watch how the variance of a 50/50 portfolio responds — the textbook
diversification curve, computed from the identity we derived.

```{python}
#| label: fig-portfolio
#| fig-cap: "Portfolio standard deviation vs. the correlation between two assets, for a 50/50 mix ($\\sigma_A=1.2\\%$, $\\sigma_B=0.9\\%$). The curve is $\\sqrt{w^2\\sigma_A^2+(1-w)^2\\sigma_B^2+2w(1-w)\\rho\\,\\sigma_A\\sigma_B}$ with $w=0.5$ — a direct reading of @eq-var-sum. Dashed grey marks the *naive* level you would get by ignoring covariance (the $\\rho=0$ value). At negative correlation the portfolio is markedly *less* volatile than either asset; this risk reduction is the covariance cross-term, made visible."
sA, sB, w = 0.012, 0.009, 0.5
rhos = np.linspace(-1, 1, 200)
port_sd = np.sqrt(w**2*sA**2 + (1-w)**2*sB**2 + 2*w*(1-w)*rhos*sA*sB)
naive = np.sqrt(w**2*sA**2 + (1-w)**2*sB**2)  # rho = 0

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(rhos, port_sd*100, color=LINE, label="portfolio SD")
ax.axhline(naive*100, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.4, label=r"naive ($\rho=0$)")
ax.axhline(sA*100, color=PURPLE, ls=":", lw=1.2, label=r"asset A SD ($1.2\%$)")
ax.axhline(sB*100, color=ACCENT, ls=":", lw=1.2, label=r"asset B SD ($0.9\%$)")
ax.set_xlabel(r"correlation $\rho_{AB}$")
ax.set_ylabel("portfolio std. dev. (%)")
ax.set_title("Diversification is the covariance term at work")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"At rho = -1:  portfolio SD = {port_sd[0]*100:.3f}%  (well below either asset)")
print(f"At rho =  0:  portfolio SD = {naive*100:.3f}%")
print(f"At rho = +1:  portfolio SD = {port_sd[-1]*100:.3f}%  (just the weighted average)")
```

The lesson is entirely in the cross-term of @eq-var-sum: when returns move *opposite* each
other ($\rho<0$), one asset's bad day is cushioned by the other's good day, and the
combined variance drops below what either asset carries alone. This is why "do they move
together" is not an academic question — it is the whole of risk management.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

Covariance and correlation are sharp tools with sharp edges. The famous traps are not bugs;
they are the definition behaving exactly as built. This section is what separates "can
compute a correlation" from "knows what it does and does not mean."

## The headline trap: zero covariance does not imply independence {#sec-zero-cov}

This is the single most-asked interview question on the topic, and the most-misunderstood.
Covariance measures **linear** co-movement. A relationship can be perfectly
*deterministic* — $Y$ a fixed function of $X$ — and still have covariance zero, because the
*linear* part of that relationship is zero.

The cleanest counterexample. Let $X$ be uniform on $[-1,1]$ — density $f(x)=\tfrac12$ on
$[-1,1]$ and zero elsewhere — and set $Y=X^2$. Then $Y$ is *completely determined* by $X$;
knowing $X$ tells you $Y$ exactly. We need two moments of $X$, both computed directly from
the [PROB-05] integral $E[X^k]=\int_{-1}^{1} x^k\cdot\tfrac12\,dx$ rather than asserted:
$$
E[X] = \int_{-1}^{1}\! x\cdot\tfrac12\,dx = \tfrac12\Big[\tfrac{x^2}{2}\Big]_{-1}^{1}
= \tfrac12\Big(\tfrac12-\tfrac12\Big) = 0,
\qquad
E[X^3] = \int_{-1}^{1}\! x^3\cdot\tfrac12\,dx = \tfrac12\Big[\tfrac{x^4}{4}\Big]_{-1}^{1}
= \tfrac12\Big(\tfrac14-\tfrac14\Big) = 0.
$$
Both vanish because the integrand is an *odd* function integrated over an interval
symmetric about $0$, so the contribution from $[-1,0]$ exactly cancels the contribution
from $[0,1]$ — and we have just seen that cancellation happen term-by-term in the
antiderivative. Now apply the shortcut @eq-cov-shortcut with $XY = X\cdot X^2 = X^3$:
$$
\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y] = E[X^3] - E[X]\,E[Y]
= 0 - 0\cdot E[Y] = 0.
$$
The covariance is exactly zero. The reason is geometric: as $X$ moves from negative to
positive, $Y=X^2$ first *falls* then *rises*, so the up-and-down cancel and there is no net
linear tilt — even though the dependence could not be tighter. @fig-zero-cov shows it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-zero-cov
#| fig-cap: "Zero covariance, total dependence. With $X$ uniform on $[-1,1]$ and $Y=X^2$, every point lies *exactly* on a parabola — $Y$ is a deterministic function of $X$ — yet the covariance is $0$ and so is the correlation. Covariance and correlation see only the *linear* tilt, and a symmetric U-shape has none. 'Uncorrelated' is therefore far weaker than 'independent': this $X$ and $Y$ are uncorrelated but maximally dependent."
rng = np.random.default_rng(1)
X = rng.uniform(-1, 1, 400)
Y = X**2
cov = np.cov(X, Y)[0, 1]
corr = np.corrcoef(X, Y)[0, 1]
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.scatter(X, Y, color=POINT, s=14, alpha=0.7)
xs = np.linspace(-1, 1, 100)
ax.plot(xs, xs**2, color=LINE, lw=1.5, alpha=0.7, label="$Y=X^2$ (exact)")
ax.set_xlabel("$X$"); ax.set_ylabel("$Y=X^2$")
ax.set_title(fr"Sample cov $= {cov:.4f}$,  corr $= {corr:.4f}$  — yet $Y$ is determined by $X$")
ax.legend(loc="upper center", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**"Uncorrelated" is strictly weaker than "independent."** Independence means the joint
factors, $f_{X,Y}=f_X f_Y$ (the subject of [PROB-08]), and it implies zero covariance — but
**the converse is false.** Zero covariance only rules out a *linear* relationship; it is
blind to curves, U-shapes, and any symmetric nonlinear dependence. A correlation of $0$ is
not a certificate of "no relationship"; it is a certificate of "no *straight-line*
relationship." There is one important family where the two notions *do* line up: the
**(multivariate) normal** distribution, where zero correlation does force full
independence. We do not prove that here — it is a property of a specific distribution we
have not yet built, so we flag it as a forward dependency on **[PROB-13]** (the normal) and
**[PROB-17]** (the multivariate normal), which derive it from the factoring of the Gaussian
density. The takeaway for now: never report "uncorrelated, therefore independent" unless you
have separately established joint normality.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Give me a pair of variables that are dependent but uncorrelated."* The $Y=X^2$ example
above is the canonical answer, and you should be able to produce it and the one-line
computation $\operatorname{Cov}=E[X^3]=0$ on demand. The follow-up is usually *"and when
does uncorrelated imply independent?"* — answer: when the pair is jointly normal, and
essentially only then among the standard models. Stating both halves is what an examiner is
listening for.
:::

## Correlation is not causation, and not the whole relationship {#sec-not-causation}

Two further honest boundaries, both flagged by Anscombe-style reasoning in [REGR-01] and
worth stating in probability terms here.

First, a nonzero correlation is a statement about the *joint distribution*, not about
mechanism. $X$ and $Y$ can be strongly correlated because $X$ drives $Y$, because $Y$
drives $X$, or because a third variable drives both (confounding). Probability theory
gives you $\rho$; it does not tell you which causal story produced it. Disentangling that
is the entire job of the Causal Inference track.

Second, even *within* linear relationships, a single correlation number is a lossy
summary. As @fig-anscombe-style shows, wildly different datasets can share the same
correlation, and correlation is fragile to outliers — one extreme point can manufacture or
destroy a correlation, because covariance, like variance, is built on *squared*-scale
products that weight far points heavily.

```{python}
#| label: fig-anscombe-style
#| fig-cap: "Correlation is a lossy, outlier-sensitive summary. **Left:** a genuine moderate linear relationship. **Middle:** pure noise with one extreme outlier that *manufactures* a spurious strong correlation out of nothing. **Right:** a clean strong relationship that one outlier *destroys*. All three carry information the single number $\\rho$ cannot. The lesson matches [REGR-01]'s Anscombe quartet: never trust a correlation without seeing the cloud."
rng = np.random.default_rng(20)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(10.5, 3.6))

# genuine moderate
x1 = rng.normal(0, 1, 60); y1 = 0.6*x1 + rng.normal(0, 0.8, 60)
# noise + one leverage outlier -> spurious correlation
x2 = rng.normal(0, 1, 60); y2 = rng.normal(0, 1, 60)
x2 = np.append(x2, 8.0); y2 = np.append(y2, 8.0)
# strong relation destroyed by an outlier
x3 = rng.normal(0, 1, 60); y3 = 0.95*x3 + rng.normal(0, 0.25, 60)
x3 = np.append(x3, 4.0); y3 = np.append(y3, -4.0)

for ax, (xx, yy, title) in zip(axes, [
        (x1, y1, "genuine moderate"),
        (x2, y2, "outlier manufactures $\\rho$"),
        (x3, y3, "outlier destroys $\\rho$")]):
    r = np.corrcoef(xx, yy)[0, 1]
    ax.scatter(xx, yy, color=POINT, s=18, alpha=0.7)
    ax.set_title(f"{title}\n$r = {r:.2f}$", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$")
plt.tight_layout()
```

## When correlation is the wrong tool, and what to reach for {#sec-alternatives}

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is Pearson correlation the right summary, and when is it not?"* The honest summary:

- **Use Pearson $\rho$** when you specifically care about a **linear** relationship between
  two quantitative variables, the cloud is roughly elliptical, and no single point dominates
  (the regime where the covariance term in @eq-var-sum is also meaningful). It is then the
  natural, scale-free measure, and the bridge to regression.
- **Do not use it** when (a) the relationship is **nonlinear** — a U-shape or threshold can
  give $\rho\approx 0$ while the variables are tightly linked (@sec-zero-cov); reach for a
  **rank correlation** (Spearman's $\rho_S$ or Kendall's $\tau$), which capture any
  *monotone* relationship, or mutual information for arbitrary dependence; (b) **outliers**
  dominate — rank correlations are far more robust; (c) the variables are **categorical** —
  use a contingency-table measure ($\chi^2$, Cramér's V) instead.
- **And never** read causation off correlation, or treat a single $\rho$ as a substitute
  for plotting the data.

The deeper point an examiner is after: Pearson correlation is the *linear* measure, and you
should know its scope is exactly that — no wider.
:::

The boundary case worth seeing once: covariance can also be **undefined**. The whole edifice
rests on $E[XY]$, $E[X]$, $E[Y]$, and the variances *existing* (being finite). For
heavy-tailed variables — the Cauchy preview from [PROB-05], where even the mean fails to
exist — covariance and correlation are simply not defined, and any software that returns a
number is reporting an artifact of the finite sample, not a population quantity. This is the
same honesty [PROB-05] insisted on for the expectation itself: the summary exists only when
the integrals converge.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from **[PROB-05 *Expectation*]** (which gave linearity and LOTUS) and
**[PROB-06 *Variance*]** (which gave $\operatorname{Var}=E[X^2]-(E[X])^2$ and standard
deviation). This article put two variables in one joint object, defined their covariance and
correlation, and proved the variance-of-a-sum identity. From here the track continues:

- **[PROB-08 *Independence and the i.i.d. Sample*]** — the converse half of @sec-zero-cov:
  *independence* (joint factors into marginals) implies zero covariance, and we make
  rigorous exactly when the implication does **not** reverse. The i.i.d. case turns
  @eq-var-sum-n into the "$\operatorname{Var}$ of a sum $=$ sum of variances" that gives the
  sample mean its $1/n$ shrink.
- **[PROB-09 *Conditional Distributions and Conditional Expectation*]** — instead of
  *summarizing* the link with one number, fully describe the distribution of $Y$ *given* a
  value of $X$, and meet $E[Y\mid X]$, the best predictor.
- **[PROB-17 *Random Vectors, Mean Vectors, and Covariance Matrices*]** — stack many
  variables into a vector; the pairwise covariances of this article become the entries of a
  **covariance matrix** $\Sigma$, with variances on the diagonal. @eq-var-sum-n becomes
  $\operatorname{Var}(\mathbf{a}^\top X)=\mathbf{a}^\top\Sigma\,\mathbf{a}\ge 0$, which is
  why $\Sigma$ is positive semidefinite.
- **[PROB-18 *Linear Transforms of Random Vectors*]** — the bilinearity proved in
  @thm-bilinearity becomes the matrix identity
  $\operatorname{Cov}(AX+b)=A\Sigma A^\top$.

**Cross-track payoff.** Regression reads the slope as
$\operatorname{Cov}(X,Y)/\operatorname{Var}(X)$ ([REGR-01]) and the variance of its estimates
through @eq-var-sum ([REGR-06]); the Geometry-of-Data track reinterprets the covariance as a
**Gram matrix** / inner product; Unsupervised Learning diagonalizes the covariance matrix in
**PCA**. The little inequality $|\operatorname{Cov}|\le\sigma_X\sigma_Y$ proved in
@thm-corr-bound is the probabilistic Cauchy–Schwarz those tracks lean on.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary sources.** Francis Galton, *Regression Towards Mediocrity in Hereditary
  Stature* (1886), where the tilt in the parent–child height cloud first cried out for a
  number; and Karl Pearson, *Mathematical Contributions to the Theory of Evolution. III.
  Regression, Heredity, and Panmixia* (1896), which gave the correlation coefficient its
  modern form. The covariance is the un-normalized core of Pearson's $r$.
- **The geometric view.** That $\rho$ is a *cosine* and covariance an *inner product* — so
  the bound $-1\le\rho\le1$ is just $|\cos\theta|\le 1$ — is the lens of the Geometry-of-Data
  track. The proof in @thm-corr-bound is the same Cauchy–Schwarz inequality reached without
  the vector-space vocabulary.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *When does uncorrelated finally mean
  independent?* — under joint normality, [PROB-13]/[PROB-17]. *How do we describe the
  relationship beyond one number?* — conditional distributions and $E[Y\mid X]$, [PROB-09].
  *What if the linear measure misses the dependence?* — rank correlations and mutual
  information, touched in @sec-alternatives and pursued in the inference and
  information-theoretic literature. *How does covariance become a matrix we can rotate?* —
  [PROB-17]/[PROB-18] and, spectrally, the Geometry-of-Data track's PCA.
